questions.learningObjective: Predict airspeed indicator behavior when the pitot tube becomes blocked during a climb
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a blocked pitot tube causes the airspeed indicator to behave like an altimeter: during a climb, indicated airspeed increases (because static pressure decreases while ram pressure in the blocked system remains trapped), falsely suggesting an increase in speed.

flashcard.term: Blocked pitot, aircraft climbing — ASI response
flashcard.definition: If the pitot tube becomes blocked (ram-air inlet) while the aircraft climbs, the ASI behaves like an altimeter: indicated airspeed INCREASES. The trapped ram-side pressure stays constant while the static-side pressure decreases with altitude, so the differential grows and the ASI reads higher even though true airspeed has not changed.
flashcard.analogy: A blocked pitot acts like a sealed bottle — its internal pressure stays the same while outside pressure drops, so the differential grows and the ASI reads faster.

jeopardy.question: If the pitot tube becomes blocked while the aircraft climbs, this is what happens to the indicated airspeed.
choices: It increases
It decreases toward zero
It remains unchanged
It oscillates randomly
jeopardy.explanation: A blocked pitot traps ram pressure. As the aircraft climbs, static pressure drops, but trapped ram pressure does not — the ASI differential grows and reads higher. The ASI effectively behaves like an altimeter.

multipleChoice.question: If the pitot tube becomes blocked while the aircraft climbs, how does the indicated airspeed respond?
choices: It increases
It decreases toward zero
It remains unchanged regardless of altitude
It oscillates randomly with no pattern
multipleChoice.explanation: A blocked pitot acts like a sealed pressure reservoir. During climb, static pressure drops but trapped ram pressure does not, so the measured differential (which the ASI displays as airspeed) grows. The ASI reads higher even though true airspeed has not changed — this is a dangerous failure that can cause pilots to unknowingly slow to stall speed while watching a 'normal' airspeed reading.

practice.question: A pilot reports that during a climb the airspeed indicator continued to read an abnormally high airspeed even as the aircraft slowed and approached a stall. The static system appears normal. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The pitot tube is blocked — trapped ram pressure masks true airspeed
The airspeed indicator has been miscalibrated at the factory before install
The aircraft was overweight for the departure runway and climb profile
The engine was developing less power than the pilot expected in climb
practice.explanation: This is the classic blocked-pitot-in-climb signature. Trapped ram pressure caused the ASI to read higher as static pressure decreased with altitude. The ASI behaves like an altimeter when the pitot inlet is blocked — reading higher as the aircraft climbs, regardless of true airspeed. The fix is to clear the pitot obstruction (often ice, in which case pitot heat should have prevented the blockage).

preassessment.question: Blocked pitot during climb — what happens to indicated airspeed?
choices: Increases
Decreases
Unchanged
Oscillates

questions.learningObjective: Predict airspeed indicator behavior when the pitot tube becomes blocked during a descent
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a blocked pitot tube during a descent causes the indicated airspeed to DECREASE as static pressure increases, because the trapped ram pressure stays constant while static pressure rises, reducing the differential and reading lower airspeed.

flashcard.term: Blocked pitot, aircraft descending — ASI response
flashcard.definition: If the pitot tube is blocked during descent, the ASI reads LOWER. The trapped ram pressure stays constant while the static pressure increases during descent, reducing the differential and decreasing the indicated airspeed — regardless of true airspeed.
flashcard.analogy: During descent, the outside pressure rises while the sealed pitot pressure stays the same — the differential shrinks and the ASI reads slower.

jeopardy.question: If the pitot tube is blocked while the aircraft descends, this is how the airspeed indicator responds.
choices: It reads higher than actual airspeed
It oscillates wildly
It reads lower than actual airspeed
It remains unchanged
jeopardy.explanation: A blocked pitot during descent: trapped ram pressure is constant, static pressure rises, differential shrinks, ASI reads lower. This is the opposite behavior from the climb case.

multipleChoice.question: If the pitot tube is blocked while the aircraft descends, how does the airspeed indicator respond?
choices: It reads higher than actual airspeed
It oscillates wildly between readings
It reads lower than actual airspeed
It remains unchanged regardless of altitude
multipleChoice.explanation: During descent with a blocked pitot, static pressure increases but trapped ram pressure stays the same, so the differential shrinks and the ASI reads lower. This can be equally dangerous — pilots may speed up trying to maintain a 'normal' airspeed reading while actually exceeding Vne.

practice.question: A pilot in descent sees the airspeed indicator reading lower than expected, even as the aircraft feels faster and the engine sounds higher. The static system checks normal. What is most likely happening?
choices: The engine is not producing enough power for the descent
The airspeed indicator has failed mechanically in flight
The pitot tube is blocked, masking true airspeed in descent
The pilot is simply over-reading the instrument scale
practice.explanation: This pattern — descent with lower-than-expected ASI but the aircraft feels fast — is the blocked-pitot-in-descent signature. Trapped ram pressure combined with increasing static pressure during descent causes the ASI to read low, potentially masking overspeed. The pilot may push speed higher to 'match' the low reading, possibly exceeding Vne.

preassessment.question: Blocked pitot during descent — ASI reads:
choices: Higher than actual
Wildly oscillating
Lower than actual
Unchanged

questions.learningObjective: Predict altimeter and VSI behavior when the static port is blocked
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a blocked static port causes the altimeter to freeze at the altitude the port was blocked at and the VSI to read zero (no rate of change), regardless of the aircraft's actual altitude changes.

flashcard.term: Blocked static port — altimeter and VSI
flashcard.definition: If the static port is blocked, the altimeter freezes at the altitude at which the blockage occurred, and the VSI reads zero regardless of actual climb or descent rate. Both instruments depend on changing static pressure to indicate altitude and rate of change; with the port sealed, no changes reach them.
flashcard.analogy: A blocked static port is like covering a barometer with a jar — the readings stop responding to the outside world.

jeopardy.question: If the static port is blocked, this is how it affects the altimeter and VSI.
choices: Altimeter reads erratically; VSI shows maximum climb rate
Altimeter freezes at the blockage altitude
Both instruments read the correct altitude and rate
Altimeter reads zero; VSI reads full scale climb
jeopardy.explanation: Blocked static: altimeter freezes (no changing static pressure to respond to), VSI reads zero (no rate of change detectable). Both instruments appear to indicate that the aircraft is in perfectly level flight at a fixed altitude, regardless of actual behavior.

multipleChoice.question: If the static port is blocked, how does it affect the altimeter and the vertical speed indicator (VSI)?
choices: The altimeter reads erratically and the VSI shows a maximum climb rate
The altimeter freezes at the altitude at which the blockage occurred
Both instruments continue to read correctly because the pitot tube compensates
The altimeter reads zero and the VSI reads full-scale climb
multipleChoice.explanation: A blocked static port cuts off the changing-pressure signal that both instruments need. Altimeter freezes. VSI reads zero. This creates a dangerous illusion of level flight at a fixed altitude — the pilot's alternate static source (if equipped) is the immediate remedy.

practice.question: During flight, a pilot notices the altimeter is stuck at 5,500 feet and the VSI reads 0 feet per minute, even though the aircraft is clearly climbing (engine at climb power, attitude indicator showing nose-up). The airspeed indicator also reads lower than expected. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The altimeter and VSI have both failed simultaneously — replace both
The static port is blocked — open the alternate static source as remedy
The aircraft's altimeter setting (Kollsman) is incorrect for the area
The pitot tube has iced up during the climb through clouds
practice.explanation: The combination of frozen altimeter + zero VSI + lower-than-expected ASI is the classic blocked-static signature. All three instruments share the static reference; blocking the port affects all three. The altimeter freezes at the blockage altitude, the VSI reads zero, and the ASI reads incorrectly (lower than actual in a climb). The alternate static source — typically inside the cabin with lower pressure than outside — is the immediate remedy until the static port can be cleared on the ground.

preassessment.question: Blocked static port — altimeter and VSI behavior:
choices: Altimeter erratic, VSI max climb
Altimeter freezes; VSI reads zero
Both read correctly
Altimeter zero; VSI full climb

questions.learningObjective: Identify the flight instruments that rely on the static port
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that three flight instruments rely on static pressure for accurate readings: the altimeter, the vertical speed indicator (VSI), and the airspeed indicator (ASI — which uses static as its reference for pitot differential).

flashcard.term: Static-port-dependent instruments
flashcard.definition: Three instruments rely on static pressure: the altimeter (senses static pressure directly to indicate altitude), the VSI (senses rate of change of static pressure to indicate climb/descent rate), and the airspeed indicator (uses static pressure as the reference against which ram pressure is compared). A blocked or leaking static port affects all three.
flashcard.analogy: The static port is the 'atmospheric reference' for three instruments. Lose the reference, and all three go wrong together.

jeopardy.question: These are the flight instruments that rely on the static port for accurate readings.
choices: Altimeter only
Attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator
Altimeter, vertical speed indicator, and airspeed indicator
Only the vertical speed indicator
jeopardy.explanation: Three pitot-static instruments: altimeter, VSI, and ASI — all use static pressure. Gyro-driven instruments (attitude, heading, turn coordinator) do not use static pressure.

multipleChoice.question: A blocked static port simultaneously disables which group of flight instruments?
choices: Altimeter only
The attitude indicator, the heading indicator, and the turn coordinator
The altimeter, the vertical speed indicator, and the airspeed indicator
Only the vertical speed indicator
multipleChoice.explanation: The altimeter, VSI, and ASI all use static pressure. A static port problem affects all three simultaneously. Gyroscopic instruments (attitude, heading, turn coordinator) are independent of the static system.

practice.question: A technician is testing the aircraft pitot-static system. The plan calls for applying a controlled vacuum to the static port and watching for correct response on the pitot-static-driven instruments. Which three instruments should the technician expect to respond to the static input?
choices: Attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator
Only the altimeter responds to controlled static input
Altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator — the static-driven trio
Engine gauges and fuel quantity gauges in the panel cluster
practice.explanation: Altimeter, VSI, and ASI all respond to static pressure changes. A functional test of the static system verifies that all three respond correctly to controlled input. Gyro and engine instruments are unrelated.

preassessment.question: Which instruments rely on the static port?
choices: Altimeter only
Gyros
Altimeter, VSI
VSI only

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary function of the pitot tube
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the pitot tube's primary function is to sense ram air pressure (the total pressure created by the aircraft's forward motion through the air), which the airspeed indicator compares to static pressure to determine airspeed.

flashcard.term: Pitot tube function
flashcard.definition: The pitot tube senses ram air pressure — the total pressure created by the aircraft moving through the air. The airspeed indicator compares this ram pressure to static pressure and displays the differential as indicated airspeed. The pitot tube is typically forward-facing and heated to prevent ice blockage.
flashcard.analogy: The pitot tube is the 'forward-facing scoop' that measures how hard the wind is pushing into it — more speed, more pressure, higher ASI reading.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary function of the pitot tube.
choices: To measure static atmospheric pressure
To sense ram air pressure for the airspeed
To measure ambient temperature
To detect icing conditions
jeopardy.explanation: The pitot tube senses ram air (total) pressure, which the ASI uses. Static pressure is measured by the separate static port. Temperature and ice detection use different sensors.

multipleChoice.question: As the aircraft moves through the air, what property does the pitot probe capture for use by the airspeed indicator?
choices: To measure static atmospheric pressure
To sense ram air pressure created by the aircraft's
To measure ambient outside air temperature
To detect icing conditions directly
multipleChoice.explanation: The pitot tube's job is to measure ram (total) pressure. The ASI takes the difference between ram and static pressures and displays it as airspeed. Static pressure is measured separately by the static port. Temperature and icing detection use other sensors.

practice.question: A technician is explaining the pitot tube to a student. Which is the simplest correct description?
choices: The pitot tube measures static atmospheric pressure at altitude
A forward-facing probe sensing ram air pressure for airspeed calculation
The pitot tube acts as a temperature probe for the air data computer
The pitot tube detects icing conditions and signals the ice protection
practice.explanation: The pitot tube is a forward-facing probe that senses the ram air pressure created by the aircraft's forward motion. The airspeed indicator compares this ram pressure to static pressure to compute indicated airspeed. The probe is typically heated to prevent ice, but ice detection itself is a separate system. Static pressure comes from the static port, not the pitot tube.

preassessment.question: What does the pitot tube do?
choices: Measure static pressure at the port
Sense ram air for the airspeed indicator
Measure outside air temperature
Detect icing conditions in cloud
preassessment.explanation: The pitot tube senses ram air pressure, which the airspeed indicator uses to compute indicated airspeed. Static pressure, temperature, and icing are sensed by other devices.

questions.learningObjective: Describe what the static pressure system senses
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the static pressure system senses the ambient (undisturbed) atmospheric pressure around the aircraft, which decreases with altitude and is used by the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator as a reference.

flashcard.term: Static pressure system
flashcard.definition: The static pressure system senses the ambient atmospheric pressure — the undisturbed outside-air pressure that decreases with altitude. Static ports are located on the fuselage in positions where airflow is undisturbed by the aircraft's motion. Static pressure is used by the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator.
flashcard.analogy: The static port measures the 'atmospheric pressure outside the airplane,' which is why all three instruments need it: altitude, rate of altitude change, and airspeed all reference it.

jeopardy.question: The static pressure system in an aircraft senses this.
choices: The aircraft's forward-motion ram air pressure
The temperature of the air
The ambient atmospheric pressure around the aircraft
The moisture content of the air
jeopardy.explanation: The static system measures undisturbed ambient atmospheric pressure. Ram pressure comes from the pitot tube. Temperature and moisture use different sensors.

multipleChoice.question: What pressure does the static system measure to drive the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed-indicator reference?
choices: The aircraft's forward-motion ram air pressure
The outside air temperature
The ambient atmospheric pressure around the aircraft
The moisture content of the air
multipleChoice.explanation: The static system senses ambient atmospheric pressure — unrelated to the aircraft's motion. Static ports are placed at locations on the fuselage where airflow is 'still' relative to the aircraft. Ram pressure (forward motion), temperature, and moisture are separate measurements.

practice.question: A technician verifying a pitot-static system calibration notes that the static port pressure changes with altitude but not with airspeed. Is this expected behavior?
choices: No — static pressure should definitely vary with airspeed too
No — static pressure should increase directly with airspeed
Yes — static pressure is altitude-dependent, airspeed-independent
Yes, but only below 10,000 feet MSL in stable air conditions
practice.explanation: Static pressure is altitude-dependent and (ideally) airspeed-independent. The static system senses ambient atmospheric pressure, which depends on altitude but not on the aircraft's speed. This is the defining characteristic of the static system. Well-designed static ports are placed where airflow around the fuselage does not disturb the measurement. This is what distinguishes static pressure from ram pressure (pitot), which depends on airspeed.

preassessment.question: What does the static pressure system sense?
choices: Ram air pressure
Temperature
Ambient atmospheric
Humidity

questions.learningObjective: Identify likely causes of an airspeed indicator that consistently reads too low
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an ASI consistently reading too low is typically caused by a partial pitot blockage (reduced ram pressure), a leak in the pitot-to-ASI plumbing, or a clogged/obstructed pitot drain that allows moisture to affect ram pressure.

flashcard.term: ASI consistently reads low — common causes
flashcard.definition: An ASI that consistently reads too low is typically caused by: (1) a partial pitot-tube blockage (ram inlet partly obstructed by dirt, insects, or ice residue), (2) a leak in the pitot-to-ASI plumbing, or (3) a clogged drain that allows moisture to affect the pitot line. The static side is less commonly a cause of consistently low readings.
flashcard.analogy: Low ASI readings = the pressure differential is smaller than it should be. Usually it's because less ram pressure is reaching the ASI.

jeopardy.question: This is a likely cause of an airspeed indicator that consistently reads too low.
choices: The airspeed indicator itself is over-calibrated
The aircraft is flying faster than rated
The altimeter is set incorrectly
A partial pitot blockage, a leak in the pitot-to-ASI
jeopardy.explanation: Consistently low ASI readings typically trace to physical issues in the pitot side of the system: partial blockage, leaks, or drain problems. Instrument miscalibration is possible but less common. Altimeter settings do not affect the ASI.

multipleChoice.question: Post-maintenance flight testing shows the airspeed indicator reading about 10 knots lower than GPS-derived speed every flight. Where should troubleshooting start?
choices: The airspeed indicator itself is over-calibrated from the factory
The aircraft is flying faster than its rated speed
The altimeter setting is incorrect
A partial pitot blockage, a pitot-line leak, or a clogged drain
multipleChoice.explanation: Consistently low ASI readings point to reduced ram pressure at the ASI — caused by partial pitot blockage, pitot-line leaks, or drain issues. Instrument miscalibration is rare. Altimeter settings have no effect on the ASI; they affect only the altimeter's Kollsman reference.

practice.question: During a test flight after pitot-static maintenance, the pilot reports the ASI now reads consistently about 10 knots slower than GPS ground speed suggests. What is the most productive first check?
choices: Replace the airspeed indicator on the bench test stand
Adjust the altimeter Kollsman setting in flight
Check the VSI calibration orifice for wear
Inspect the pitot tube and plumbing for blockage or leaks
practice.explanation: After recent maintenance, a consistently low ASI is usually traceable to the pitot side — partial obstruction (dirt, insects, residue), a loose fitting, a missed O-ring, or a blocked pitot drain. Inspect the physical pitot path first. Instrument replacement and altimeter/VSI adjustments are premature without evidence they are needed.

preassessment.question: ASI reads too low — likely cause?
choices: Over-calibration
Aircraft flying too fast
Wrong altimeter setting
Partial pitot blockage

questions.learningObjective: Identify the likely cause of a VSI that shows a constant rate of descent in level flight
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a VSI showing constant descent in level flight is most likely caused by a slow leak in the static line to the VSI, a clogged VSI calibration orifice, or a miscalibrated instrument — rather than an actual aircraft problem.

flashcard.term: VSI shows constant descent in level flight — cause
flashcard.definition: A VSI that shows a constant rate of descent even in level flight is most likely caused by a slow leak in the VSI's static line (slow pressure equalization creates an apparent rate), a clogged VSI calibration orifice (which produces non-zero output at steady state), or instrument miscalibration. A small leak is the most common cause in the field.
flashcard.analogy: A constant false descent reading means the VSI is 'seeing' a slow pressure change that isn't actually there — usually a leak that never quite equalizes.

jeopardy.question: This is the most likely cause of a VSI showing a constant rate of descent even in level flight.
choices: The aircraft is actually descending without the pilot noticing
The altimeter is set incorrectly
The pitot tube is heated
A slow leak in the VSI's static line, a clogged orifice, or miscalibration
jeopardy.explanation: Constant descent in level flight is almost always a VSI system problem. Slow static-line leak is the most common in the field. Altimeter setting does not affect the VSI. Pitot heat is unrelated.

multipleChoice.question: A vertical speed indicator (VSI) shows a constant rate of descent even in level flight. Which is the most likely cause?
choices: The aircraft is actually in a slow, constant descent that the pilot has not noticed
The altimeter is set to the wrong local pressure
The pitot tube has been heated
A slow leak in the VSI's static line, a clogged calibration orifice, or a miscalibrated instrument
multipleChoice.explanation: A constant false descent indication in level flight is a classic symptom of a VSI system issue — usually a slow static-line leak that never equalizes, or a clogged calibration orifice that produces non-zero output at steady state. The altimeter would confirm actual altitude; if the altimeter is stable the aircraft is in level flight, ruling out option A.

practice.question: A technician is verifying a VSI that reads a constant 100 fpm descent even when the aircraft is level and the altimeter is stable. What is the most productive first check?
choices: Replace the VSI indicator immediately on the bench
Adjust the aircraft's weight and balance before the next flight
Reset the transponder to clear any stored faults
Perform a static-system leak check to find the most common cause
practice.explanation: A static-system leak check is the standard first step. A small leak in the VSI's static line allows pressure to slowly equalize through the leak path instead of through the calibration orifice, producing a false constant-rate reading. If the static system checks tight, the next step is bench checking the VSI's calibration orifice. Weight/balance and transponder resets are unrelated.

preassessment.question: VSI shows constant descent in level flight — likely cause?
choices: Aircraft actually descending
Wrong altimeter setting
Pitot heat on
Static line leak, clogged orifice

questions.learningObjective: Describe what an altimeter measures
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an altimeter measures atmospheric pressure and converts it to altitude using a standard pressure-altitude relationship, allowing the pilot to fly at known altitudes relative to a reference (sea level or a set local pressure).

flashcard.term: Altimeter
flashcard.definition: The altimeter measures static atmospheric pressure and converts it to altitude using a standard pressure-altitude relationship. The pilot sets a local altimeter setting (Kollsman window) so the indicated altitude is referenced appropriately (MSL above sea level). Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude at a known rate (approximately 1 inch Hg per 1,000 feet at low altitudes).
flashcard.analogy: An altimeter is a pressure-sensing barometer calibrated to display the height that corresponds to the sensed pressure.

jeopardy.question: This is what an altimeter measures.
choices: Ram air pressure for airspeed
Atmospheric (static) pressure
Aircraft attitude
Aircraft heading
jeopardy.explanation: The altimeter measures static atmospheric pressure and displays it as altitude. Ram pressure is used by the ASI. Attitude and heading are gyroscopic/magnetic instrument measurements.

multipleChoice.question: An altimeter displays altitude to the pilot. What physical quantity is the instrument actually sensing to produce that readout?
choices: Ram air pressure for the airspeed indicator
Atmospheric (static) pressure
Aircraft pitch and bank attitude
Aircraft heading relative to magnetic north
multipleChoice.explanation: An altimeter senses static atmospheric pressure and translates it into altitude. Lower pressure means higher altitude. Ram pressure, attitude, and heading are measured by other instruments with different operating principles.

practice.question: A student asks how an altimeter 'knows' how high the aircraft is. What is the best explanation?
choices: The onboard GPS receiver tells the altimeter the current altitude
It measures static atmospheric pressure, which decreases with altitude
It measures radar altitude above the terrain directly below the aircraft
It uses the aircraft's inertial navigation system for vertical reference
practice.explanation: The classic altimeter is a pressure instrument. Static atmospheric pressure decreases predictably with altitude at a known rate (about 1 inch Hg per 1,000 feet near sea level). The instrument converts that pressure to altitude using a standard scale, with the local altimeter setting adjusting the reference for current weather. Radar altimeters are separate devices measuring height above terrain directly. GPS altitude is independent. Inertial systems integrate acceleration but are not part of a pressure altimeter.

preassessment.question: What does an altimeter measure?
choices: Ram air pressure
Atmospheric static
Attitude
Heading

questions.learningObjective: Describe how a pilot sets the altimeter before flight
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that before flight the pilot sets the altimeter by rotating the Kollsman knob to match the current local altimeter setting (usually reported by ATIS, AWOS, or ATC) so that the altimeter indicates field elevation when the aircraft is on the ground.

flashcard.term: Altimeter pre-flight setting
flashcard.definition: Before flight the pilot sets the altimeter's Kollsman window to the current local altimeter setting — a barometric pressure reported in inches of mercury (inHg) or hectopascals (hPa). When set correctly, the altimeter reads field elevation when the aircraft is on the ground. Current settings are available from ATIS, AWOS, or ATC.
flashcard.analogy: Setting the altimeter is like calibrating a scale to a known weight before using it — you need a reference to make the reading accurate.

jeopardy.question: Before flight, this is how the altimeter is set.
choices: To the current local altimeter setting (from ATIS
To zero, regardless of altitude
To standard atmosphere (29.92 inHg) for all flights
To whatever reading the previous pilot left it at
jeopardy.explanation: The altimeter is set to the current local altimeter setting before flight. This produces a reading of field elevation on the ground. Standard (29.92) is used only at and above FL180 in US airspace (transition altitude). Zeroing or using a previous setting are both incorrect.

multipleChoice.question: Before flight, a pilot sets the altimeter to:
choices: The current local altimeter setting (obtained from ATIS
Zero, regardless of actual altitude
Standard atmosphere (29.92 inHg) for every flight
Whatever the previous pilot left it at
multipleChoice.explanation: The pre-flight altimeter setting is the current local barometric pressure, which makes the altimeter read field elevation on the ground. ATIS, AWOS, and ATC provide this. Standard (29.92) is used only at and above the transition altitude (FL180 in the US). Zeroing and keeping prior settings are both incorrect.

practice.question: A pilot tunes the local ATIS and hears 'altimeter 30.07.' Before flight, how should the pilot set the altimeter?
choices: Set the Kollsman window to 30.07 and cross-check with field elevation
Set it to 29.92 inHg for the standard atmosphere reference
Leave it at whatever setting the previous pilot last used
Set the indicator to zero before starting the takeoff roll
practice.explanation: The pilot sets the Kollsman window to the reported value (30.07 inHg). When correctly set, the altimeter should indicate field elevation while the aircraft is on the ground. Verify this against the published field elevation as a cross-check. A large discrepancy points to an altimeter problem that must be investigated before flight. Standard setting (29.92) is used above the transition altitude, not on the ground.

preassessment.question: Before flight, the altimeter is set to:
choices: Current local altimeter setting
Zero
29.92 standard
Whatever the last pilot set

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary purpose of the vertical speed indicator (VSI)
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the vertical speed indicator displays the aircraft's rate of climb or descent (in feet per minute), derived from the rate of change of static pressure.

flashcard.term: VSI purpose
flashcard.definition: The vertical speed indicator (VSI) displays the aircraft's rate of climb or descent in feet per minute (fpm), derived by sensing the rate of change of static atmospheric pressure. Positive readings indicate climb; negative readings indicate descent; zero indicates level flight.
flashcard.analogy: The VSI is a 'first derivative' of altitude — it shows how fast the altitude is changing, not the altitude itself.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary purpose of the vertical speed indicator (VSI).
choices: To display the aircraft's current altitude
To display the aircraft's airspeed
To display the aircraft's heading
To display the aircraft's rate of climb or descent
jeopardy.explanation: The VSI shows rate of altitude change, not absolute altitude (that's the altimeter), airspeed (ASI), or heading (heading indicator).

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of the vertical speed indicator (VSI)?
choices: To display the aircraft's current altitude
To display the aircraft's airspeed
To display the aircraft's heading relative to magnetic north
To display the aircraft's rate of climb or descent
multipleChoice.explanation: The VSI shows the rate at which altitude is changing. Zero reading = level flight. Positive = climbing; negative = descending. It is the 'first derivative' of the altimeter's altitude display.

practice.question: A pilot is flying a constant-rate descent of 500 feet per minute to reach the initial approach fix at the correct altitude. Which instrument most directly helps the pilot maintain this rate?
choices: The altimeter, which shows the current altitude directly
The airspeed indicator, which shows forward speed only
The heading indicator, which shows magnetic direction
The VSI, which directly displays rate of descent in fpm
practice.explanation: The VSI directly displays rate, which is exactly the parameter being controlled. The pilot maintains the desired rate by keeping the VSI needle at 500 fpm down. The altimeter shows current altitude but not rate. Airspeed and heading are separate parameters. Pilots manage descent profiles primarily by VSI reference with altimeter cross-checks.

preassessment.question: What does the VSI show?
choices: Current indicated altitude
Current indicated airspeed
Current magnetic heading
Rate of climb or descent
preassessment.explanation: The VSI shows the rate of climb or descent in feet per minute.

questions.learningObjective: Identify a flight instrument commonly powered by the aircraft's vacuum system
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the attitude indicator (and heading indicator, in some installations) in traditional general-aviation aircraft is driven by a gyroscope powered by the engine-driven vacuum pump, using suction to spin the gyro at high RPM.

flashcard.term: Vacuum-driven gyroscopic instruments
flashcard.definition: In traditional general-aviation aircraft, the attitude indicator — and often the heading indicator (directional gyro) — is driven by a vacuum-powered gyroscope. An engine-driven vacuum pump provides suction that spins the internal gyro at high RPM, maintaining its stable reference. Turn coordinators are more commonly electric.
flashcard.analogy: Vacuum pumps suck air through the gyro housing, making the rotor spin like a pinwheel — fast enough to maintain rigidity in space.

jeopardy.question: This flight instrument is commonly powered by the aircraft's vacuum system in traditional GA aircraft.
choices: Airspeed indicator
Altimeter
Magnetic compass
Attitude indicator
jeopardy.explanation: The attitude indicator (and often the heading indicator) in traditional GA is vacuum-driven. The ASI and altimeter are pressure instruments. The magnetic compass is passive — no power needed.

multipleChoice.question: On a traditional GA instrument panel, the engine-driven vacuum pump keeps one particular gyroscopic indicator spinning. Which indicator?
choices: Airspeed indicator
Altimeter
Magnetic compass
Attitude indicator
multipleChoice.explanation: Traditional attitude indicators and heading indicators are vacuum-driven gyroscopes. The vacuum pump provides suction that spins the internal gyros. Airspeed indicator and altimeter are pressure-based (pitot-static) instruments. The magnetic compass is a passive instrument that uses Earth's magnetic field.

practice.question: A pilot reports that the attitude indicator is slowly becoming unreliable while the altimeter and airspeed indicator continue working correctly. Which component is most likely to be the problem?
choices: The pitot-static system feeding the ASI and altimeter
The magnetic compass mounted on the glareshield
The panel-mounted GPS navigator
The engine-driven vacuum pump or its regulator
practice.explanation: A degrading attitude indicator with healthy pitot-static instruments points to the vacuum system. The attitude indicator is vacuum-driven, while the altimeter and ASI are independent pitot-static instruments. Vacuum pumps are wear items; a failing pump causes gradual gyro degradation. Compass and GPS are independent systems.

preassessment.question: Which instrument uses the aircraft vacuum system?
choices: ASI
Altimeter
Magnetic compass
Attitude indicator

questions.learningObjective: Identify the gyroscopic principle that allows a gyroscopic instrument to maintain a stable reference
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that rigidity in space is the gyroscopic principle that allows a gyroscope to maintain a stable spatial reference — once spinning, the gyro resists changes to its axis orientation unless a force is applied.

flashcard.term: Rigidity in space (gyroscopic principle)
flashcard.definition: Rigidity in space is the gyroscopic principle that allows a spinning gyroscope to maintain a stable spatial reference. The spinning mass resists changes to the orientation of its spin axis — unless an external force causes precession. This stability is what allows gyros to provide attitude, heading, and turn references even as the aircraft maneuvers around them.
flashcard.analogy: A spinning top resists being tipped over — the same physics keeps gyro instruments stable while the aircraft pitches, rolls, and yaws around them.

jeopardy.question: This gyroscopic principle allows gyroscopic instruments to maintain a stable reference.
choices: Rigidity in space
Precession
Angular momentum only when accelerating
Brownian motion
jeopardy.explanation: Rigidity in space is the correct term. Precession is a related but different phenomenon (the response of a spinning gyro to an applied force). 'Angular momentum only when accelerating' is not a valid physical concept. Brownian motion is unrelated (random thermal motion).

multipleChoice.question: A spinning gyroscope holds the orientation of its axis steady even as the aircraft rolls and pitches around it. Which physical principle is responsible?
choices: Rigidity in space
Precession
Angular momentum only when accelerating
Brownian motion
multipleChoice.explanation: Rigidity in space is the key principle for gyroscopic stability. Once spinning, the gyro resists changes to its axis orientation. Precession is a different but related concept — it describes how a spinning gyroscope responds to an applied force (the response appears 90 degrees around the rotation from where the force was applied). Both principles matter, but stability comes from rigidity.

practice.question: A student pilot asks how an attitude indicator 'knows' which way is up, even during aggressive maneuvering. What is the correct explanation?
choices: A spinning gyroscope maintains rigidity in space as a stable reference
The instrument uses an internal accelerometer array to sense gravity
The instrument uses GPS signals to calculate the aircraft's attitude
The instrument measures atmospheric pressure in multiple directions
practice.explanation: The traditional attitude indicator uses a gyroscope exhibiting rigidity in space. The gyro's axis of rotation maintains a stable spatial reference — the aircraft structure pitches and rolls around the gyro while the gyro itself remains oriented to its original axis, giving the pilot a visual reference for attitude. The gyro is initially aligned with Earth's local vertical, and its own inertia keeps it there while the aircraft maneuvers. Modern AHRS replaces the mechanical gyro with solid-state sensors but operates on analogous principles.

preassessment.question: What principle makes gyroscopes maintain a stable reference?
choices: Rigidity in space
Precession
Angular momentum only while accelerating
Brownian motion

questions.learningObjective: Identify the flight instruments that appear in the standard 'T' flight instrument arrangement
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the standard 'T' flight instrument arrangement places the airspeed indicator on the left, the attitude indicator on top center, the altimeter on the right, and the heading indicator on the bottom center of the top row, forming a 'T' shape with the attitude indicator at the top and the other three arranged around it.

flashcard.term: Standard 'T' instrument arrangement
flashcard.definition: The standard 'T' arrangement places: airspeed indicator (left), attitude indicator (top center), altimeter (right), and heading indicator (bottom center, directly below the attitude indicator). This forms a 'T' shape and is the FAA-preferred layout because the pilot's primary scan flows naturally around the attitude indicator.
flashcard.analogy: The 'T' puts the attitude indicator at the intersection, with airspeed, altitude, and heading radiating outward — the pilot's primary reference at the center.

jeopardy.question: These are the four instruments that appear in the standard 'T' flight instrument arrangement.
choices: Airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, and heading indicator
Attitude indicator, heading indicator, turn coordinator, VSI
Altimeter, ASI, VSI, and turn coordinator
Attitude indicator, altimeter, magnetic compass, and engine tachometer
jeopardy.explanation: The 'T' arrangement places the airspeed indicator (left), attitude indicator (top center), altimeter (right), and heading indicator (bottom center). Other standard-six instruments (VSI and turn coordinator) are in the lower row but not in the 'T' itself.

multipleChoice.question: Which four instruments appear in the standard 'T' flight instrument arrangement?
choices: Airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, and heading indicator
Attitude indicator, heading indicator, turn coordinator, and VSI
Altimeter, airspeed indicator, VSI, and turn coordinator
Attitude indicator, altimeter, magnetic compass, and engine tachometer
multipleChoice.explanation: The 'T' consists of ASI (left), attitude indicator (top center), altimeter (right), and heading indicator (bottom center). This layout puts the primary-scan instrument (attitude) at the center and allows a natural scan pattern. VSI and turn coordinator are part of the classic 'standard six' but are positioned below the 'T' proper.

practice.question: A new technician looking at a standard six-pack instrument panel sees a cross or 'T' shape formed by four of the six instruments. Which four are they?
choices: ASI, attitude indicator, altimeter, heading indicator — the FAA-preferred T
Attitude indicator, VSI, heading indicator, and the turn coordinator instrument
The four engine gauges commonly grouped together on the lower right panel
VSI, turn coordinator, altimeter, and engine tachometer in a T arrangement
practice.explanation: The 'T' arrangement is FAA-preferred because it supports a natural scan pattern centered on the attitude indicator: Airspeed indicator (upper left), attitude indicator (upper center), altimeter (upper right), and heading indicator (center below the attitude indicator). The other two standard-six instruments (VSI and turn coordinator) fill the bottom row but are not part of the 'T' itself.

preassessment.question: Which instruments form the standard 'T' arrangement?
choices: ASI, attitude indicator, altimeter, heading
AI, HI, turn coordinator, VSI
Altimeter, ASI, VSI, turn coordinator
AI, altimeter, compass, tachometer

questions.learningObjective: Identify which standard flight instrument is NOT driven by a gyroscope
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the magnetic compass is NOT a gyroscopic instrument — it uses magnetic alignment to Earth's magnetic field — while the attitude indicator, heading indicator (directional gyro), and turn coordinator ARE gyroscopic.

flashcard.term: Non-gyroscopic flight instruments
flashcard.definition: The magnetic compass is NOT a gyroscopic instrument — it is a magnetic instrument that senses the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field. The three traditional gyroscopic instruments are: attitude indicator, heading indicator (directional gyro), and turn coordinator/turn-and-bank indicator.
flashcard.analogy: Magnetic compass: passive, no moving power-driven parts. Gyros: spinning rotors powered by vacuum or electric.

jeopardy.question: This flight instrument is NOT typically driven by a gyroscope.
choices: Attitude indicator
Magnetic compass
Heading indicator (directional gyro)
Turn coordinator
jeopardy.explanation: The magnetic compass aligns to Earth's magnetic field — no gyroscope required. The attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator are the three gyroscopic instruments.

multipleChoice.question: Which of the following flight instruments is NOT typically driven by a gyroscope?
choices: The attitude indicator
The magnetic compass
The heading indicator (directional gyro)
The turn coordinator
multipleChoice.explanation: The magnetic compass is a passive magnetic instrument — it senses the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field via a pivoting magnet assembly. The other three listed instruments are gyroscopic: the attitude indicator uses a gyro to indicate pitch and roll, the heading indicator uses a gyro aligned to the compass for stable heading, and the turn coordinator uses a canted gyro to sense yaw rate.

practice.question: A pilot's checklist calls for verifying 'no gyroscopic instruments have unusual indications before flight.' Which instrument is the pilot NOT checking?
choices: The attitude indicator
The magnetic compass
The heading indicator
The turn coordinator
practice.explanation: The three gyro instruments (AI, HI, turn coordinator) are part of a gyro check. The magnetic compass is not gyroscopic — it is checked separately for correct reading, readability, and fluid clarity.

preassessment.question: Which instrument is NOT typically driven by a gyroscope?
choices: Attitude indicator
Magnetic compass
Heading indicator
Turn coordinator

questions.learningObjective: Describe what the Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS) in an EFIS uses solid-state sensors to determine
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the AHRS in an EFIS uses solid-state sensors (typically MEMS gyros, accelerometers, and magnetometers) to determine aircraft attitude (pitch and roll) and heading, replacing traditional mechanical gyroscopic instruments.

flashcard.term: AHRS in EFIS
flashcard.definition: The Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS) uses solid-state MEMS sensors — three-axis gyros, accelerometers, and magnetometers — to determine aircraft pitch, roll, and heading. The AHRS provides this data to the EFIS displays, replacing the mechanical gyroscopes used in traditional attitude and heading indicators.
flashcard.analogy: AHRS is a 'solid-state gyro' — same function as the mechanical gyros, but implemented with tiny silicon sensors that don't wear out.

jeopardy.question: The Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS) in an EFIS uses solid-state sensors to determine this.
choices: Only airspeed
Aircraft position relative to runways
Aircraft pitch, roll (attitude), and heading
Aircraft weight and balance
jeopardy.explanation: AHRS provides pitch, roll, and heading — the functions traditionally done by vacuum-driven attitude and heading indicators. Position is determined by GPS. Airspeed comes from the air data computer (pitot-static). Weight and balance is not a sensed quantity.

multipleChoice.question: The Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS) in an EFIS uses solid-state sensors to determine:
choices: Airspeed only
Aircraft position relative to airport runways
Aircraft pitch, roll (attitude), and heading
Aircraft weight and balance
multipleChoice.explanation: The AHRS is the solid-state replacement for traditional mechanical gyroscopes. Three-axis MEMS gyros measure rotation rates, accelerometers sense the gravity vector for pitch/roll, and magnetometers provide heading reference. The combined data yields pitch, roll, and heading for the EFIS displays.

practice.question: A technician explains the AHRS to a student by comparing it to a traditional attitude indicator. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: AHRS is a mechanical gyro just like the traditional vacuum unit
AHRS only works when GPS signals are continuously available
AHRS uses solid-state MEMS sensors instead of mechanical gyros
AHRS measures airspeed instead of aircraft attitude and heading
practice.explanation: AHRS replaces the mechanical vacuum-driven gyros of traditional attitude and heading indicators with solid-state MEMS sensors (gyros, accelerometers, magnetometers). It provides the same pitch, roll, and heading information but without wear parts, warm-up time, or a vacuum pump. GPS is a separate input but is not required for AHRS operation — AHRS provides attitude and heading on its own.

preassessment.question: AHRS uses solid-state sensors to determine:
choices: Indicated airspeed only
Runway position on field
Pitch, roll, and heading
Aircraft weight and balance
preassessment.explanation: An AHRS (Attitude and Heading Reference System) uses solid-state sensors (accelerometers, rate gyros, magnetometers) to determine aircraft pitch, roll, and heading.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the source of airspeed and altitude data shown on an EFIS Primary Flight Display
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the airspeed and altitude data on an EFIS Primary Flight Display (PFD) come from the Air Data Computer (ADC), which senses pitot and static pressures via solid-state sensors and computes indicated airspeed, altitude, and related parameters for display.

flashcard.term: EFIS airspeed and altitude source
flashcard.definition: In an EFIS, airspeed and altitude on the Primary Flight Display come from the Air Data Computer (ADC). The ADC senses pitot and static pressures with solid-state pressure sensors and computes indicated airspeed, altitude, VSI, and often true airspeed and Mach. The PFD simply displays the ADC's output.
flashcard.analogy: The ADC is to a glass cockpit what mechanical altimeters, ASIs, and VSIs are to an analog panel — but all in one solid-state box, driving the glass display.

jeopardy.question: In an EFIS, this is the source of the airspeed and altitude shown on the Primary Flight Display (PFD).
choices: The Air Data Computer
GPS receiver
The magnetic compass
The transponder
jeopardy.explanation: The ADC is the source of airspeed and altitude in an EFIS. GPS provides position and ground speed but not indicated airspeed or pressure altitude. The magnetic compass provides heading reference. The transponder reports altitude to ATC but does not generate it.

multipleChoice.question: In a glass-cockpit EFIS, the primary flight display's airspeed and altitude data come from:
choices: The Air Data Computer
The GPS receiver
The magnetic compass
The transponder
multipleChoice.explanation: The ADC is the single source of airspeed and altitude on the PFD. It senses pitot and static pressures, computes indicated airspeed, pressure altitude, and VSI, and provides the data to the PFD. GPS contributes position and ground speed (shown separately), and the magnetic compass contributes heading, but airspeed and altitude come from the ADC.

practice.question: A technician troubleshooting a glass-cockpit PFD sees that airspeed and altitude displays are frozen while the heading tape and GPS position are updating normally. Which component is the most likely source of the problem?
choices: The Air Data Computer or its pitot-static input
The AHRS driving the heading display tape
The magnetic compass correction card
The transponder altitude encoder
practice.explanation: Airspeed and altitude both come from the ADC, so a single ADC or pitot-static problem can freeze both while leaving heading (from AHRS) and GPS position unaffected. Check ADC power, data output, and pitot-static plumbing. The AHRS drives the working heading tape, and GPS drives the working position, so neither is the cause.

preassessment.question: Where does the EFIS PFD get its airspeed and altitude data?
choices: Air Data Computer
GPS
Magnetic compass
Transponder

questions.learningObjective: Identify the appropriate next step when a pitot heat circuit breaker pops immediately upon reset
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that when a pitot heat circuit breaker pops immediately upon reset, the most appropriate next step is to leave it OFF and investigate for a short circuit or damaged pitot heater element — not to repeatedly reset the breaker, which risks wiring damage or fire.

flashcard.term: Pitot heat CB pops on reset — next step
flashcard.definition: When a pitot heat circuit breaker trips immediately upon reset, it indicates an electrical fault — short circuit in the wiring, damaged heating element, or moisture intrusion. The correct next step is to leave the breaker OFF and investigate the cause. Repeatedly resetting the breaker can damage wiring, cause arcing, or start a fire.
flashcard.analogy: A breaker that trips immediately on reset is saying 'there's still a fault.' Forcing it repeatedly is like holding an alarm silent while the fire burns.

jeopardy.question: When a pitot heat circuit breaker pops immediately upon reset, this is the most appropriate next step.
choices: Reset the breaker repeatedly until it holds
Replace the breaker with a larger-rated one
Leave it OFF and investigate for a short circuit
Bypass the breaker temporarily to use the pitot heat
jeopardy.explanation: Immediate re-trip indicates a persistent fault. Leave the breaker off and investigate. Resetting repeatedly, up-sizing, or bypassing are all dangerous and can damage wiring or start a fire.

multipleChoice.question: A technician resets a popped pitot-heat circuit breaker and it trips again immediately. What is the correct next action?
choices: Reset the breaker repeatedly until it finally holds
Replace the circuit breaker with a larger-rated one
Leave the breaker OFF and investigate for a short circuit
Bypass the breaker with a jumper to use the pitot heat
multipleChoice.explanation: Immediate re-trip means the fault is still present. Repeated reset attempts can damage the wiring, cause arcing, or start a fire. Up-sizing the breaker defeats the overcurrent protection and is strictly prohibited. Bypassing the breaker is equally unsafe. The correct approach is to leave it off and find the cause — short in the wiring, failed heating element, or moisture are the most common.

practice.question: During pre-flight, a pilot resets a tripped pitot heat circuit breaker and it immediately pops again. What should the technician or pilot do?
choices: Reset it a third time — if it still pops, reset a fourth time
Install a higher-rated circuit breaker in that position
Leave the breaker OFF and investigate the cause before resetting
Jumper the breaker closed for the remainder of the flight
practice.explanation: Immediate re-trip is the circuit protection doing its job. Leave it OFF and investigate. Likely causes include a short in the pitot heat wiring (often from chafing), a failed or water-intruded heating element, or a damaged connector. The aircraft should not be flown into conditions requiring pitot heat until the fault is corrected. Flight into known icing without working pitot heat is unsafe. Find and fix the root cause before further reset.

preassessment.question: Pitot heat CB pops immediately on reset — next step?
choices: Reset the breaker again
Install a larger breaker
Leave OFF and investigate
Jumper the breaker closed
preassessment.explanation: A breaker that trips immediately on reset indicates an active fault. Leave the circuit OFF and investigate before any further action.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the flight instrument powered by the aircraft's vacuum system in a conventional GA cockpit
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that in a conventional general-aviation cockpit, the attitude indicator (and typically the heading indicator) is driven by the engine-driven vacuum pump, which spins an internal gyroscope. The airspeed indicator and altimeter are pitot-static pressure instruments, and the magnetic compass is a passive magnetic device, none of which use the vacuum system.

flashcard.term: Gyro spun by the engine-driven pump
flashcard.definition: On a conventional piston single, the attitude indicator runs on suction pulled through the case by the engine-driven pump, which spins the gyro rotor up to operating RPM. The directional gyro usually shares the same vacuum supply. By contrast, the turn coordinator is almost always electrically driven so one failure does not take all three gyros at once.
flashcard.analogy: The vacuum pump is like a vacuum cleaner pulling air through a pinwheel inside the attitude indicator — the faster the air moves through, the faster the gyro spins.

jeopardy.question: On a steam-gauge piston trainer, this is the gyro instrument spun by the engine-driven vacuum pump.
choices: Turn coordinator
Airspeed indicator
Altimeter
Attitude indicator
jeopardy.explanation: The attitude indicator is the classic vacuum-driven instrument in GA aircraft, along with the heading indicator. The airspeed indicator and altimeter are pitot-static pressure instruments. The turn coordinator is usually electric, not vacuum driven.

multipleChoice.question: A steam-gauge piston trainer has an engine-driven vacuum pump. Which gyro instrument typically runs off that pump?
choices: Attitude indicator — internal gyroscope spun by engine-driven vacuum airflow
Airspeed indicator, which uses vacuum to sense ram air pressure in the line
Altimeter, which uses applied vacuum to flex its aneroid bellows capsule
Turn coordinator, which relies on engine vacuum for its canted internal gyro
multipleChoice.explanation: The attitude indicator is the classic vacuum-driven gyro, along with the heading indicator in most installations. The ASI and altimeter are pressure instruments (no vacuum). The turn coordinator is typically electric in modern GA aircraft.

practice.question: A technician is explaining to a student which instruments stop working when the engine-driven vacuum pump fails in a conventional Cessna. Which instrument is most directly affected?
choices: The magnetic compass, because vacuum stabilizes the compass card during turns
The airspeed indicator, because vacuum drives the pitot pressure differential
The altimeter, because vacuum is needed to reference static pressure properly
The attitude indicator — its gyroscope is vacuum-driven; HI typically also affected
practice.explanation: A vacuum pump failure takes out the attitude indicator and heading indicator together because both rely on vacuum-driven gyros. The airspeed indicator and altimeter are pressure instruments and keep working. The magnetic compass is passive and independent of the vacuum system.

preassessment.question: Identify the gyro instrument typically driven by the engine-driven vacuum pump.
choices: Altimeter
Airspeed indicator
Attitude indicator
Turn coordinator

questions.targetCaetStem: Which flight instrument is commonly powered by the aircraft’s vacuum system?

questions.learningObjective: Distinguish between a turn coordinator and a turn & bank indicator in a small GA aircraft
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a turn coordinator has a canted gyro (tilted roughly 30 degrees) and therefore shows both rate of roll and rate of turn, while a turn & bank indicator has a horizontally mounted gyro and shows only rate of turn.

flashcard.term: Turn coordinator vs turn & bank
flashcard.definition: The turn coordinator uses a gyro canted about 30 degrees from horizontal, so it responds to BOTH rate of roll and rate of turn — useful for detecting the onset of a bank before the turn develops. The turn & bank (needle-and-ball) uses a horizontal gyro and shows ONLY rate of turn. Both include an inclinometer (ball) for coordination, but the turn coordinator is strictly better for picking up roll rate during entry into a turn.
flashcard.analogy: Turn coordinator = canted gyro sees both the roll-in and the turn; turn & bank = horizontal gyro sees only the finished turn.

jeopardy.question: For a light GA ship, this correctly distinguishes a turn coordinator from the classic turn & bank.
choices: They display the same information with only cosmetic face differences
The canted gyro senses roll rate AND turn rate; turn & bank senses turn only
The turn & bank senses rate of roll; the turn coordinator does not
Both sense only rate of roll
jeopardy.explanation: The key difference is the gyro orientation. A canted gyro (turn coordinator) responds to both roll rate and turn rate; a horizontal gyro (turn & bank) responds only to rate of turn. The turn coordinator gives the pilot an earlier cue during roll-in.

multipleChoice.question: A turn coordinator and a classic turn & bank display similar information but are not identical. Identify the correct distinction between the two for a light GA ship.
choices: They are identical instruments with different face markings
The turn & bank has a canted gyro that senses roll rate; the turn coordinator has a horizontal gyro that senses only yaw
Neither instrument senses roll rate; both show only heading change
The turn coordinator has a canted gyro that senses rate of roll AND rate of turn, while the turn & bank has a horizontal gyro that senses rate of turn only
multipleChoice.explanation: The defining difference is gyro orientation. The turn coordinator uses a gyro tilted about 30 degrees, which makes it respond to both roll and turn rates. The turn & bank uses a horizontal gyro that responds only to turn rate. The turn coordinator gives an earlier indication when the aircraft first starts to roll, before the turn is established.

practice.question: A technician is explaining to a student why the turn coordinator in a Cessna 172 has a canted gyro while the turn & bank in an older Piper does not. Which is the most accurate explanation?
choices: The canted gyro is simply a cosmetic design change with no function
A horizontal gyro senses both roll and yaw; canting removes roll sense
Canting 30 degrees adds roll-rate sensing for earlier turn-entry cueing
Canted gyros are used only in IFR-certified aircraft configurations
practice.explanation: The canted-gyro design is the reason a turn coordinator detects roll rate before the turn develops. Canting the gyro about 30 degrees makes it sense rate of roll in addition to rate of turn, so the turn coordinator cues the pilot earlier during roll-in. The horizontal gyro of the turn & bank responds only after a turn has actually started. Both instruments include a slip/skid ball, but the gyro-response characteristics are the key functional difference.

preassessment.question: Turn coordinator vs turn & bank — key difference?
choices: They are identical instruments in every way
Turn & bank senses roll; turn coordinator does not
Neither one of them senses any rate of roll
TC senses roll and turn; T&B senses turn only
preassessment.explanation: The turn coordinator senses both rate of roll and rate of turn via its canted gyro. The older turn-and-bank senses only rate of turn. Option B inverts the relationship.

questions.targetCaetStem: Which statement best describes the difference between a turn coordinator and a turn & bank indicator in a small GA aircraft?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the gyroscopic principle that allows gyroscopic instruments to maintain a stable reference
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that rigidity in space is the gyroscopic principle that allows a spinning gyroscope to maintain a stable reference — the spinning mass resists changes to the orientation of its spin axis, letting the attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator maintain a reliable reference while the aircraft maneuvers around them.

flashcard.term: Physical property a gyro exploits for reference
flashcard.definition: Rigidity in space is the phenomenon where a spinning mass resists any change to the direction of its spin axis. The rotor keeps pointing the same way while the airframe rolls, pitches, and yaws around it, giving the instrument its steady reference. Precession describes a different behavior — how the gyro responds to an applied force, with the effect showing up 90 degrees around in the direction of rotation.
flashcard.analogy: A spinning top stays upright for the same reason — angular momentum keeps the spin axis rigid relative to space, even as forces try to tip it.

jeopardy.question: This gyroscopic principle allows a gyroscope to maintain a stable reference while the aircraft maneuvers around it.
choices: Rigidity in space
Magnetic dip
Coriolis effect
Precession
jeopardy.explanation: Rigidity in space describes a spinning gyro's resistance to changes in the orientation of its spin axis. Precession describes how the gyro responds to an applied force. Magnetic dip and Coriolis are unrelated to gyroscopic stability.

multipleChoice.question: What physical property of a spinning mass is exploited by gyro instruments to hold a fixed reference?
choices: Precession — the response of a spinning gyro to an applied external force
Magnetic dip — the downward tilt of Earth's magnetic field lines with latitude
Rigidity in space — a spinning gyro resists changes to its spin axis orientation
Coriolis effect — the apparent deflection of motion caused by Earth's own rotation
multipleChoice.explanation: Rigidity in space is the principle: a spinning mass resists changes in the orientation of its axis. Precession is a different property (response to applied force, offset 90 degrees). Magnetic dip affects compasses. Coriolis is unrelated.

practice.question: A student asks how an attitude indicator can keep showing level flight even while the aircraft pitches and rolls. What is the correct explanation?
choices: The instrument senses gravity with a small accelerometer package
The instrument uses GPS signals to determine aircraft attitude
The instrument senses aircraft structural bending during maneuvers
A spinning gyroscope inside maintains rigidity in space as a reference
practice.explanation: The gyroscope resists changes to the orientation of its spin axis (rigidity in space), so it stays fixed relative to its original direction while the aircraft moves around it. The instrument face displays that fixed reference as an artificial horizon. The gyro is initially aligned with local vertical, and its own inertia keeps it there while the aircraft pitches and rolls around it. Modern AHRS replaces the mechanical gyro with solid-state sensors computing an equivalent reference.

preassessment.question: Which principle gives gyros a stable reference?
choices: Precession
Magnetic dip
Coriolis effect
Rigidity in space

questions.targetCaetStem: Which principle allows gyroscopic instruments to maintain a stable reference?

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary function of the vacuum system in a vacuum-driven attitude indicator
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that in a vacuum-driven attitude indicator the vacuum system's PRIMARY function is to spin the internal gyroscope — the engine-driven vacuum pump pulls air through the instrument case, and the moving air strikes buckets on the gyro rotor, spinning it to high RPM so that rigidity in space gives a stable attitude reference.

flashcard.term: Suction system's job in an air-driven attitude indicator
flashcard.definition: The pump pulls cabin air through the instrument case, and that moving airstream strikes cups machined into the rotor to spin it up to operating RPM. Once the gyro is running, its rigidity in space provides the steady attitude reference the pilot flies. Everything else about the instrument is mechanical linkage between that rotor and the face of the ball.
flashcard.analogy: Imagine blowing across a pinwheel — the airflow spins the wheel. The vacuum pump is pulling the air instead of blowing it, but the result is the same: the gyro spins.

jeopardy.question: On an air-driven attitude indicator, this is the MAIN job performed by the suction system.
choices: It powers the standby backup battery for the panel
It cools the instrument housing and nearby electronics
It erects the attitude indicator to magnetic north on start
It spins the gyro by pulling air across the rotor buckets
jeopardy.explanation: The vacuum pump's primary role is to spin the gyro by pulling air through the case and across the rotor buckets. Cooling, heading erection to magnetic north, and battery charging are not functions of the vacuum system.

multipleChoice.question: For an air-driven attitude indicator, the suction system's MAIN job is to:
choices: Cools the instrument housing to prevent internal gyro overheating
Drives the gyro by airflow so rigidity provides an attitude reference
Erects the attitude indicator toward magnetic north at startup
Powers the instrument's backup battery during alternator failure
multipleChoice.explanation: The vacuum system's primary function is to spin the gyro. The engine-driven vacuum pump pulls air through the instrument case and across buckets on the gyro rotor, driving it at high RPM. A spinning gyro exhibits rigidity in space, which is what gives the attitude indicator its stable reference. Cooling, magnetic erection, and battery power are not vacuum-system functions.

practice.question: A technician is explaining to a student why the attitude indicator takes a few minutes to become reliable after engine start in a vacuum-driven system. What is the correct explanation?
choices: The vacuum pump must spin the gyro up to RPM before rigidity is stable
The attitude instrument needs time to warm up electrically before becoming accurate
The GPS signal must stabilize before the attitude indicator can compute pitch and roll
The magnetic compass must align with the gyro before the instrument becomes usable
practice.explanation: The lag is mechanical: the gyro needs to reach operating RPM before it has enough angular momentum for a stable reference. Until it is spinning fast enough, rigidity in space is too weak to hold attitude. There is no electrical warm-up, no GPS dependency, and no compass alignment in a classic vacuum-driven AI.

preassessment.question: For an air-driven attitude indicator, the suction system MAINLY:
choices: Erects to magnetic north
Cools the housing
Spins the gyroscope
Powers the backup battery

questions.targetCaetStem: In a vacuum-driven attitude indicator, the vacuum system PRIMARILY:

questions.learningObjective: Identify the flight instruments that depend on the static port for accurate readings
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that three flight instruments depend on the static port for accurate readings: the altimeter (uses static pressure directly), the vertical speed indicator (uses rate of change of static pressure), and the airspeed indicator (uses static pressure as the reference against ram pressure from the pitot tube).

flashcard.term: Cockpit instruments that share the static line
flashcard.definition: Three round gauges tap into the same static supply: the altimeter, the VSI, and the airspeed indicator. The altimeter reads static pressure directly to produce altitude, the VSI senses the rate at which that pressure is changing to produce climb or descent rate, and the ASI references static against pitot ram pressure. Any obstruction at the port therefore upsets all three gauges at once.
flashcard.analogy: The static port is the 'reference pressure' shared by three instruments — lose the reference, and all three lose accuracy at once.

jeopardy.question: A blockage at the static port affects these three cockpit instruments together.
choices: Altimeter only
Attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator
Magnetic compass and tachometer
Altimeter, vertical speed indicator, and airspeed indicator
jeopardy.explanation: Altimeter, VSI, and ASI all rely on static pressure. Gyroscopic instruments and the magnetic compass do not. A static port problem affects all three pitot-static instruments at once.

multipleChoice.question: A blockage at the static port would cause inaccurate indications on which cockpit instruments?
choices: The altimeter only, because it reads static pressure directly
The attitude indicator, the heading indicator, and the turn coordinator
The magnetic compass and the engine tachometer
The altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator — all use static pressure
multipleChoice.explanation: Three instruments depend on the static port: altimeter (senses static pressure directly), VSI (senses rate of change of static pressure), and ASI (uses static pressure as the reference against ram pressure). Gyroscopic instruments, the magnetic compass, and engine instruments are independent of the static system. A blocked static port therefore affects all three pitot-static instruments at the same time.

practice.question: A technician pressurizes the static side of a pitot-static test setup and sees the altimeter and VSI respond, but the airspeed indicator also registers a change even though the pitot side is untouched. Is this expected behavior?
choices: Yes — the ASI is static-dependent like the altimeter and VSI
No — the VSI should not be affected by static pressure changes
No — the airspeed indicator should be unaffected by the static side
Yes, but only the altimeter should have responded to the change
practice.explanation: The airspeed indicator is static-dependent because it uses static pressure as the reference against pitot ram pressure. The airspeed indicator compares pitot pressure against static pressure — changing the static reference changes the measured differential. The altimeter, VSI, and ASI are all static-dependent, which is why all three responded.

preassessment.question: Which instruments rely on the static port?
choices: Altimeter, VSI, and ASI
Gyroscopic instruments
Altimeter only
Compass and tachometer

questions.targetCaetStem: Which flight instruments rely on the static port for accurate readings?

questions.learningObjective: Identify what an Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS) uses solid-state sensors to measure
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS) typically uses solid-state sensors — MEMS rate gyros, accelerometers, magnetometers, and pressure sensors — to measure aircraft attitude (pitch and roll), heading, and air data (altitude, airspeed, VSI) for display on the PFD.

flashcard.term: What EFIS solid-state sensors pick up
flashcard.definition: Inside an EFIS suite the sensing chain is split across MEMS rate gyros and accelerometers (for pitch, roll, and rotation), magnetometers (for heading), and pressure transducers in the Air Data Computer (for pitot, static, airspeed, altitude, and VSI). Everything the PFD shows the pilot about attitude, direction, and air data ultimately comes from one of those solid-state devices.
flashcard.analogy: EFIS solid-state sensors are the digital equivalent of the entire analog six-pack: attitude, heading, airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed — all from silicon sensors instead of spinning gyros and aneroid wafers.

jeopardy.question: On an EFIS, the solid-state sensors pick up these flight parameters in place of mechanical gyros and pitot capsules.
choices: Only the aircraft heading from a magnetometer
Attitude, heading, and air data from solid-state sensors
Only engine performance data from engine sensors
Only GPS position from the installed GPS receiver
jeopardy.explanation: EFIS uses solid-state sensors to measure aircraft attitude (pitch and roll), heading, and air data (altitude, airspeed, VSI). Engine performance is handled by the engine instrument system (not EFIS). GPS position is from the GPS receiver, not the EFIS sensor suite.

multipleChoice.question: On an EFIS, solid-state sensors replace mechanical gyros and pitot capsules in order to measure which parameters?
choices: Only aircraft heading, sensed by a solid-state magnetometer module
Only the aircraft's GPS position and track from satellite signals
Only engine performance parameters such as RPM and fuel flow
Attitude, heading, and air data from MEMS gyros and ADC sensors
multipleChoice.explanation: EFIS solid-state sensors measure attitude (pitch and roll), heading, and air data (altitude, airspeed, and vertical speed). The AHRS side uses MEMS rate gyros, accelerometers, and magnetometers to produce pitch, roll, and heading. The Air Data Computer uses solid-state pressure sensors on the pitot and static lines to produce altitude, airspeed, and VSI. Engine data and GPS position come from separate subsystems.

practice.question: A technician is explaining to an apprentice which parameters an EFIS derives from its own solid-state sensor suite (excluding external inputs like GPS). Which list is correct?
choices: Attitude, heading, altitude, airspeed, and VSI from AHRS and ADC
Heading and outside air temperature only from the sensor suite
GPS position, ground speed, and track across the ground
Engine RPM, fuel flow, and other engine-monitor parameters
practice.explanation: The EFIS solid-state sensors produce attitude (pitch and roll), heading, altitude, airspeed, and vertical speed — from the combined AHRS and Air Data Computer sensor suite. AHRS handles attitude and heading; ADC handles airspeed, altitude, and VSI. GPS position and engine parameters are external inputs that the EFIS may display but does not generate from its own sensors.

preassessment.question: EFIS solid-state sensors measure:
choices: Only magnetic heading data
Only engine performance parameters
Attitude, heading, and air data
Only onboard GPS position data
preassessment.explanation: EFIS solid-state sensor suites measure attitude, heading, and air data (altitude, airspeed, vertical speed) to drive the primary and multifunction displays.

questions.targetCaetStem: An Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS) typically uses solid-state sensors to measure:

questions.learningObjective: Identify the instrument primarily affected when the pitot tube becomes completely blocked
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that when the pitot tube becomes completely blocked the airspeed indicator is the instrument primarily affected, because the ASI is the only instrument that uses ram pressure from the pitot tube. The altimeter, VSI, and gyro instruments remain unaffected by a pitot-only blockage as long as the static side is clear.

flashcard.term: Primary instrument affected by pitot-tube blockage
flashcard.definition: A complete pitot-tube blockage primarily affects the airspeed indicator. The ASI is the only instrument that relies on ram pressure from the pitot tube. The altimeter and VSI are static-pressure instruments and keep working normally. Gyroscopic instruments (attitude, heading, turn coordinator) are independent of the pitot-static system entirely.
flashcard.analogy: The pitot tube feeds only the ASI. Block it, and the ASI is the instrument that goes wrong; the others keep reading normally.

jeopardy.question: Of the round gauges, this is the one that takes the direct hit when the pitot tube is obstructed.
choices: The altimeter
The vertical speed indicator
The airspeed indicator
The attitude indicator
jeopardy.explanation: A complete pitot blockage affects only the ASI because the ASI is the single instrument that uses ram pressure from the pitot tube. The altimeter and VSI remain normal (static-only). Gyro instruments are independent of the pitot-static system.

multipleChoice.question: If the pitot tube is obstructed, which cockpit gauge is the one most directly affected by the blockage?
choices: The airspeed indicator, because it is the only instrument that uses ram pressure from the pitot tube
