The vertical speed indicator, because it needs pitot pressure to measure rate of climb
The attitude indicator, because pitot ice affects gyro alignment
The altimeter, because it depends on pitot pressure for its barometric reference
multipleChoice.explanation: The airspeed indicator is the only instrument affected by a complete pitot tube blockage. The ASI uses ram pressure from the pitot tube as part of its differential measurement; blocking the pitot freezes the ram side. The altimeter and VSI are static-only instruments and keep reading normally. Gyroscopic instruments (attitude, heading, turn coordinator) are entirely independent of the pitot-static system.

practice.question: During flight a pilot notes that the altimeter and VSI are reading normally, but the airspeed indicator is behaving erratically after flying through visible moisture. Which system is the most likely culprit?
choices: The pitot tube — only the ASI uses ram pressure; static is confirmed healthy
The vacuum pump, because the ASI is a vacuum-driven instrument in most aircraft
The magnetic compass, because compass deviations affect the pitot sensing path
The static system, because any erratic airspeed always indicates a static problem
practice.explanation: Normal altimeter and VSI, erratic ASI only, points to a pitot-only problem. The ASI is the sole user of ram pressure. Static-dependent instruments are normal, so static is fine. Pitot icing or moisture intrusion is a common cause.

preassessment.question: Pitot tube completely blocked — which instrument primarily affected?
choices: Altimeter
VSI
Attitude indicator
Airspeed indicator

questions.targetCaetStem: Which instrument is primarily affected if the pitot tube becomes completely blocked?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the most likely cause of an airspeed indicator that consistently reads too low
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that when a pilot reports the airspeed indicator consistently reads too low, the most likely cause is a partial pitot-tube blockage (reduced ram pressure) or a leak in the pitot line, because a reduced differential between ram and static pressure produces a consistently low ASI reading.

flashcard.term: ASI consistently reads low — most likely cause
flashcard.definition: When the airspeed indicator consistently reads too low, the most likely cause is a partial pitot blockage or a leak in the pitot line. Either condition reduces the ram pressure reaching the ASI, which shrinks the pitot-static differential and produces a low indicated airspeed. A clogged pitot drain is a related cause. Static-side problems are a less common cause of consistently low ASI readings.
flashcard.analogy: Less ram pressure reaching the ASI = smaller differential = lower indicated airspeed. Look for a partial block or a leak in the pitot line first.

jeopardy.question: A pilot squawks that the ASI always indicates a value lower than expected — this is the most probable root cause.
choices: A defective altimeter setting knob
An over-calibrated airspeed indicator from the factory
A partial pitot blockage or leak reducing ram pressure
An incorrect compass card entry
jeopardy.explanation: Consistently low ASI readings point to reduced ram pressure — typically a partial pitot block or a leak in the pitot line that reduces ram pressure reaching the ASI. Altimeter setting and compass cards are unrelated. Instrument over-calibration is rare compared to physical pitot-path problems.

multipleChoice.question: Scenario: a pilot squawks that the ASI always indicates a value lower than expected. Identify the most probable root cause.
choices: A defective altimeter Kollsman setting knob on the front of the instrument
An over-calibrated airspeed indicator from the factory before installation
An incorrect entry on the aircraft's compass deviation correction card
A partial pitot blockage or line leak that reduces ram-pressure differential
multipleChoice.explanation: Consistently low ASI readings come from reduced ram pressure reaching the instrument. The most common causes are a partial pitot blockage (dirt, insects, ice residue, moisture) or a leak in the pitot line between the tube and the ASI, which reduces the pitot-static differential. A clogged pitot drain is related. Altimeter and compass settings have no effect on the ASI.

practice.question: During a post-maintenance test flight a pilot reports the airspeed indicator consistently reads about 8 knots lower than GPS ground speed suggests. Which is the most productive first check?
choices: Replace the airspeed indicator on the bench without further checks
Inspect the pitot tube and line for blockage or a loose fitting
Reset the transponder and altimeter to clear any stored faults
Replace the magnetic compass card with a newly calibrated one
practice.explanation: After maintenance, newly low ASI readings are often traceable to work done on the pitot side — a loose fitting, missed O-ring, partial blockage, or pinched line. Reduced ram pressure is the most likely cause of a consistently low ASI. Inspect the physical pitot path first. Instrument replacement, transponder/altimeter resets, and compass changes are premature without evidence they are needed.

preassessment.question: ASI consistently reads too low — most likely cause?
choices: Altimeter Kollsman knob
Partial pitot block or leak
Over-calibrated ASI unit
Compass card deviation
preassessment.explanation: A partial pitot blockage or leak reduces sensed ram pressure, so the ASI reads low. Altimeter settings, calibration bias, and compass error do not match this symptom.

questions.targetCaetStem: A pilot reports the airspeed indicator consistently reads too low. Which of the following is the MOST likely cause?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the most appropriate next step when a pitot heat circuit breaker pops immediately upon reset
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that when the pitot heat circuit breaker pops immediately upon reset, the most appropriate next step is to leave the breaker OFF and investigate the cause (short circuit, damaged heater element, moisture intrusion). Repeatedly resetting, replacing with a larger-rated breaker, or bypassing the breaker are unsafe actions that can damage wiring or start a fire.

flashcard.term: Pitot heat CB pops immediately on reset — correct action
flashcard.definition: When the pitot heat circuit breaker trips immediately upon reset, the fault is still present. The correct action is to leave the breaker OFF and investigate — check for a short in the heater wiring, a damaged heating element, or moisture intrusion. Resetting again risks wiring damage or fire. Installing a larger-rated breaker defeats overcurrent protection and is prohibited. Bypassing the breaker is equally unsafe.
flashcard.analogy: A breaker tripping on reset is saying 'the fault is still here.' Forcing it closed is like taping over a smoke alarm while the fire still burns.

jeopardy.question: On the pitot heat circuit the breaker trips the instant the tech resets it — this is the correct follow-up action.
choices: Reset the breaker repeatedly until it finally holds for flight
Install a larger-rated circuit breaker in the affected panel
Bypass the breaker with a jumper wire for the planned flight
Leave the breaker OFF and investigate for short or damage
jeopardy.explanation: Immediate retrip means the fault is still present. Leave the breaker off and find the cause. Repeated reset, upsizing, or bypassing are unsafe and violate maintenance practice.

multipleChoice.question: On the pitot heat circuit, the breaker trips the instant the technician resets it. What is the correct follow-up action?
choices: Reset the breaker repeatedly until it finally holds in position
Install a larger-rated circuit breaker so it will no longer trip out
Leave OFF and investigate short, heater damage, or moisture first
Bypass the breaker with a jumper so pitot heat is usable this flight
multipleChoice.explanation: An immediate retrip means the fault is still there. The breaker is doing its job. Leave it off and find the root cause — most commonly a short in the heater wiring, a water-intruded heater element, or a damaged connector. Repeatedly resetting can damage wiring or start a fire. Up-sizing the breaker defeats overcurrent protection. Bypassing is equally unsafe. The aircraft should not be flown into conditions requiring pitot heat until corrected.

practice.question: A technician on the ramp resets a tripped pitot heat breaker during pre-flight and it pops again within a second. The pilot is preparing to fly into known icing conditions. What is the correct response?
choices: Reset once more; if it pops, delay one hour and try resetting again
Install a larger-rated breaker to prevent the pitot heat from tripping
Jumper the breaker closed so pitot heat works for this one flight only
Leave the breaker OFF and investigate the cause before any flight
practice.explanation: Immediate retrip is the circuit protection doing its job — the fault is still present. Leave the breaker OFF and investigate. Likely causes include a short in the heater wiring (often from chafing), a failed or water-intruded heater element, or a damaged connector. The aircraft should not be dispatched into icing without working pitot heat. Flight into known icing without working pitot heat is unsafe because pitot icing while airborne is a real threat. Bypassing or up-sizing is prohibited.

preassessment.question: Pitot heat CB pops on reset — next step?
choices: Reset repeatedly
Install larger breaker
Leave OFF and investigate
Bypass with jumper

questions.targetCaetStem: Scenario: The pitot heat circuit breaker pops immediately upon reset. Which of the following is the MOST APPROPRIATE next step?

questions.learningObjective: Describe what an altimeter measures
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an altimeter measures atmospheric (static) pressure and converts it to altitude using a standard pressure-altitude relationship, with the Kollsman setting adjusting the reference for current local barometric pressure.

flashcard.term: What the altimeter actually senses
flashcard.definition: The instrument reads ambient static pressure and displays altitude using the standard pressure-altitude curve. Because pressure drops predictably with height (roughly 1 inHg for every thousand feet near sea level), the conversion is reliable. The Kollsman window allows the pilot to dial in a local altimeter setting so the instrument reads field elevation on the ground.
flashcard.analogy: An altimeter is basically a pressure-sensing barometer with a scale labeled in feet instead of inHg.

jeopardy.question: Inside the altimeter, this is the physical quantity the instrument actually senses.
choices: Static pressure, converted to altitude
The aircraft's magnetic heading data
Ram air pressure at the pitot tube
The aircraft's pitch and bank attitude
jeopardy.explanation: An altimeter measures static atmospheric pressure and converts it to an altitude reading. Ram pressure is used by the ASI. Pitch/bank is measured by the attitude indicator. Heading is measured by the heading indicator or compass.

multipleChoice.question: The altimeter is calibrated to display altitude based on which sensed quantity?
choices: Ram air pressure sensed at the pitot tube, which is converted to airspeed
Static atmospheric pressure, converted to altitude via standard pressure laws
Aircraft pitch and bank attitude measured relative to the visible horizon
Aircraft heading relative to magnetic north as sensed by the magnetic compass
multipleChoice.explanation: The altimeter senses static atmospheric pressure and translates it into altitude. Lower pressure means higher altitude. The Kollsman setting adjusts the local barometric reference. Ram pressure, attitude, and heading use different instruments.

practice.question: A student asks how an altimeter 'knows' the aircraft's altitude without using GPS or radar. Which is the best explanation?
choices: The altimeter senses static pressure and converts it to altitude
The altimeter uses an internal accelerometer to track vertical motion
The altimeter receives altitude data from a dedicated radar altimeter
The altimeter reads pitch angle and estimates altitude from climb rate
practice.explanation: A standard altimeter is a pressure instrument. It measures static atmospheric pressure, which decreases predictably with altitude (about 1 inHg per 1,000 ft near sea level); the instrument converts that pressure to a displayed altitude using a standard scale, with the Kollsman setting accounting for current weather. Radar altimeters are separate devices that measure height above terrain directly. Inertial or accelerometer-based altitude tracking is not how a pressure altimeter works.

preassessment.question: The altimeter reads altitude based on which sensed quantity?
choices: Ram air pressure from the pitot tube
Static atmospheric pressure from the ports
Pitch and bank angle from the attitude gyro
Magnetic heading from the flux gate sensor
preassessment.explanation: The altimeter senses static atmospheric pressure and converts it to an altitude reading. Ram pressure is the airspeed indicator. Pitch and bank come from the attitude indicator; heading from the compass or heading indicator.

questions.learningObjective: Describe what the static pressure system in an aircraft senses
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the static pressure system senses the undisturbed ambient atmospheric pressure around the aircraft — a pressure that decreases with altitude and is independent of the aircraft's forward speed — and supplies this reference to the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator.

flashcard.term: What the static pressure system senses
flashcard.definition: The static pressure system senses the undisturbed ambient atmospheric pressure around the aircraft. This pressure decreases with altitude and is independent of the aircraft's forward motion. Static ports are placed where airflow around the fuselage is undisturbed by the aircraft's movement. The altimeter, VSI, and ASI all reference static pressure.
flashcard.analogy: The static port is the 'atmospheric pressure reading' of the surrounding air — altitude-dependent, speed-independent.

jeopardy.question: Sampled at the flush fuselage ports and fed to the altimeter, ASI, and VSI — this is what gets measured.
choices: Ram air pressure created by the aircraft's forward motion
Humidity level of the surrounding outside air mass
Outside air temperature at the current altitude level
Ambient atmospheric pressure, decreasing with altitude
jeopardy.explanation: The static system senses undisturbed ambient atmospheric pressure — altitude-dependent and speed-independent. Ram pressure comes from the pitot tube. Temperature and humidity use separate sensors.

multipleChoice.question: An aircraft static pressure system detects which quantity?
choices: Undisturbed ambient atmospheric pressure, decreasing with altitude
Ram air pressure created by the aircraft's forward motion
The outside air temperature at the current flight altitude
The ambient humidity level of the surrounding air
multipleChoice.explanation: The static pressure system senses ambient atmospheric pressure, which depends on altitude but not on the aircraft's forward motion. Static ports are placed where airflow is 'still' relative to the aircraft so the measurement is unaffected by motion. Ram pressure, temperature, and humidity are measured by separate sensors.

practice.question: A technician verifying a static system calibration notes that the port pressure changes with altitude but not with airspeed. Is this the expected behavior for a well-designed static system?
choices: No — static pressure should increase with increasing airspeed
Yes, but only below 10,000 feet density altitude
No — static pressure should decrease with increasing airspeed
Yes — static pressure depends on altitude, not on airspeed
practice.explanation: Static pressure is altitude-dependent and ideally airspeed-independent. The static system is designed to sense ambient atmospheric pressure, which depends on altitude but not on the aircraft's speed. Well-designed static ports are placed where local airflow does not disturb the reading. This is exactly what distinguishes the static system from the pitot tube, which does depend on airspeed (because it senses ram pressure).

preassessment.question: The static pressure system senses:
choices: Ram air pressure
Cabin humidity level
Outside air temperature value
Undisturbed ambient pressure
preassessment.explanation: The static pressure system senses undisturbed ambient atmospheric pressure, which drives the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator.

questions.targetCaetStem: The static pressure system in an aircraft senses:

questions.learningObjective: Identify the standard atmospheric pressure value at sea level
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is 29.92 inches of mercury (inHg), equivalent to 1013.25 hectopascals (hPa) or about 14.7 psi. This is the reference pressure used for flight-level operations at and above the transition altitude.

flashcard.term: ISA sea level pressure
flashcard.definition: The International Standard Atmosphere fixes mean sea level pressure at 29.92 inHg, which is the same as 1013.25 hPa or roughly 14.7 psi. In US airspace, pilots dial this value into the Kollsman window at and above the transition altitude (FL180) for flight level operations. Below FL180 the altimeter gets set to the local baro setting broadcast on ATIS or advised by ATC.
flashcard.analogy: Think of 29.92 inHg as the 'global default' for altimeters when the aircraft is high enough that local weather pressures aren't used for altimetry.

jeopardy.question: Under ISA, this value is the accepted pressure at mean sea level.
choices: 28.00 inHg (948 hPa)
jeopardy.explanation: Standard sea-level pressure is 29.92 inHg / 1013.25 hPa / 14.7 psi. The other values are plausible but incorrect distractors, either below or above the standard.

multipleChoice.question: State the standard (ISA) pressure value accepted at sea level.
choices: 29.92 inches mercury (1013.25 hPa, ~14.7 psi)
28.00 inches mercury (about 948 hPa)
30.12 inches mercury (about 1020 hPa)
32.50 inches mercury (about 1100 hPa)
multipleChoice.explanation: The standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is 29.92 inHg, equivalent to 1013.25 hPa (millibars) or approximately 14.7 psi. This is the reference pressure pilots set on the altimeter at and above the transition altitude (FL180 in the US) for flight-level operations.

practice.question: A technician is explaining the standard altimeter setting used above the transition altitude. Which value is correct?
choices: 28.00 inHg, which represents typical low-pressure weather systems
29.92 inHg (1013.25 hPa) — international standard pressure reference
30.12 inHg, which represents typical high-pressure weather systems
32.50 inHg, which is used only at very high altitudes worldwide
practice.explanation: Standard sea-level pressure — 29.92 inHg, equivalent to 1013.25 hPa or about 14.7 psi — is the international reference used by all aircraft at and above the transition altitude, so every aircraft on flight levels shares the same altimetry reference. Below the transition altitude, aircraft use the local altimeter setting. The other values represent actual weather highs and lows but are not the standard.

preassessment.question: ISA sea level pressure value?
choices: 28.00 inHg
29.92 inHg
30.12 inHg
32.50 inHg
preassessment.explanation: ISA sea-level pressure is 29.92 inHg (1013.25 hPa).

questions.targetCaetStem: What is the standard atmospheric pressure at sea level?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the traditional flight instruments that the Primary Flight Display (PFD) typically replaces in a modern glass cockpit
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that in a modern glass cockpit the Primary Flight Display (PFD) typically replaces the traditional airspeed indicator, altimeter, vertical speed indicator, attitude indicator, and heading indicator — the five instruments of the standard six-pack that together represent primary flight parameters. The PFD draws airspeed, altitude, and VSI from the Air Data Computer and attitude/heading from the AHRS.

flashcard.term: Round dials consolidated by the PFD
flashcard.definition: On a modern glass cockpit setup, five of the traditional round-dial instruments collapse onto the PFD: attitude, altitude, airspeed, vertical speed, and heading. The underlying data sources are the AHRS (attitude, heading) and the Air Data Computer (airspeed, altitude, VSI). Shops commonly retain a backup magnetic compass and a standalone turn coordinator as independent backups to the glass suite.
flashcard.analogy: The PFD is a digital 'six-pack minus the turn coordinator' — one screen that shows airspeed, altitude, VSI, attitude, and heading at once.

jeopardy.question: Inside a glass panel setup, these are the conventional round dials consolidated onto the PFD.
choices: Only the attitude indicator and heading indicator are consolidated
Airspeed, altimeter, VSI, attitude, and heading indicators combined
Only the airspeed indicator and altimeter are moved to the PFD
Only the magnetic compass and turn coordinator are consolidated
jeopardy.explanation: The PFD consolidates five traditional instruments: ASI, altimeter, VSI, attitude indicator, and heading indicator. The turn coordinator and magnetic compass are often retained as backups.

multipleChoice.question: Inside a glass panel setup, the PFD consolidates which set of conventional round dials onto a single screen?
choices: ASI, altimeter, VSI, attitude indicator, and heading indicator combined
Only the airspeed indicator and altimeter, with other instruments unchanged
Only the attitude indicator and heading indicator, displayed on the PFD
Only the magnetic compass and turn coordinator, which become digitized
multipleChoice.explanation: The PFD replaces five traditional instruments: the airspeed indicator, altimeter, VSI, attitude indicator, and heading indicator. Airspeed, altitude, and VSI come from the Air Data Computer; attitude and heading come from the AHRS. The turn coordinator and magnetic compass are typically retained as mechanical backups.

practice.question: A technician is explaining to a pilot transitioning from analog to glass which mechanical instruments are consolidated onto the PFD in a typical modern cockpit. Which list is correct?
choices: Attitude and heading only — air-data instruments remain mechanical gauges
Only the airspeed indicator and altimeter — all others remain mechanical
ASI, altimeter, VSI, attitude, and heading — the five primary flight gauges
Only the magnetic compass and turn coordinator — others remain mechanical
practice.explanation: The PFD typically consolidates the five primary flight instruments — airspeed indicator, altimeter, VSI, attitude indicator, and heading indicator — into a single display. These parameters come from the Air Data Computer (airspeed/altitude/VSI) and the AHRS (attitude/heading). A mechanical turn coordinator and magnetic compass are usually retained as backup per regulatory requirements, and are not part of what the PFD replaces.

preassessment.question: What does the PFD typically replace in a glass cockpit?
choices: AI and HI only
ASI and altimeter only
ASI, altimeter, VSI, AI, and HI
Compass and turn coordinator only

questions.targetCaetStem: In a modern glass cockpit, the Primary Flight Display (PFD) typically replaces which traditional flight instruments?
